notes: simple-ui-change (L1/Low): Both versions score 100%. The skill adds procedural consistency but the without-skill version provides deeper code exploration (DataTable, PaginationParams). Assertions don't differentiate quality here.

cross-feature-change (L3/Medium): Skill version scores 100% vs 78% without. Key differentiators: (1) skill ensures explicit pause for user review, (2) skill prompts analysis of existing ACs from feature docs. However, without-skill version provides better risk analysis (urgency vs priority confusion, case sensitivity, audit logging).

global-infra-change (L4/High): Skill version scores 100% vs 90% without. Only difference: existing AC listing. Both produce excellent rollback strategies, phased implementation, and comprehensive testing. Without-skill version has MORE detailed risk mitigation and MORE tests (21 vs 11).

Overall: Skill adds most value for L3+ changes where it enforces review gates and AC analysis. For L1 changes, skill value is minimal. The biggest gap is in ensuring the agent pauses for user review at the right complexity level.
Both versions struggle with the same weakness when without skill: not pausing for review and not analyzing existing ACs. These are the unique contributions of the skill.
